content_preview.title: IISc Team Clocks Electrons Flowing as a Near-Perfect Dirac Fluid in Ultraclean Graphene, Violating Wiedemann-Franz Law by More Than 200-Fold
content_preview.summary: Researchers at the Indian Institute of Science report a quantum-critical electron fluid in graphene with viscosity near the theoretical lower bound and a dramatic decoupling of heat and charge transport.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

A team at the Indian Institute of Science (IISc) has reported direct measurements of a long-predicted exotic state in graphene in which electrons abandon their usual particle-like behavior and instead flow as a nearly frictionless quantum liquid. The work, published in *Nature Physics* and highlighted this week by [ScienceDaily](https://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2026/04/260415042152.htm), shows that charge and heat transport in ultraclean graphene decouple by more than two order...

editor_notes.content_quality: Strong condensed-matter physics article on IISc Dirac fluid measurement in ultraclean graphene.
editor_notes.source_verification: ScienceDaily VERIFIED via WebFetch: IISc + NIMS Japan collaboration, 200-fold Wiedemann-Franz deviation at low temperatures, Dirac-point fluid-like behavior, Nature Physics publication (vol 21 issue 9, DOI 10.1038/s41567-025-02972-z), water-like low viscosity all confirmed.
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: All technical claims verified. Original Nature Physics paper dated 2025; ScienceDaily release is April 15 2026 coverage.
editor_notes.originality: No prior Dirac fluid or Wiedemann-Franz coverage.
